county_metadata.record_losses: Three courthouse fires in January 1872, November 1872, and January 1873 destroyed virtually all county records EXCEPT probate and equity court records. Deeds, mortgages, land records, and court judgments were lost. Probate Court records (1782-1950) including will books, bonds, inventories, and appraisements survived. Equity Court records (1820-1868) also survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Ninety Six District (one of six counties carved from it). Operated as a district until 1868 when SC Constitution converted districts to counties. Pre-1785 records are in Ninety Six District files at SC Archives.
county_metadata.research_tips: Because of the triple fires, Abbeville requires creative workarounds. Start with the surviving probate and equity records — FamilySearch has Wills & Misc. Records (catalog #201856) and Abstracts of Old Ninety-Six and Abbeville District Wills and Bonds (catalog #2044476). Equity Records of Old 96 and Abbeville District Vol. 1, 1791-1849 (catalog #637391) are invaluable for pre-fire deed substitutes. State land grant plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov can partially reconstruct the lost deed chain. Erskine College Archives in Due West has local family records tied to the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church. The Abbeville District Historical Association (PO Box 578, McCormick, SC 29835) may have compiled abstracts. Marriage registers survived: catalog #1152450.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. Tax assessor property records available via qPublic. In-person access at Register of Deeds: 102 Court Square, Room 103, Abbeville, SC 29620 (864-366-5312).
county_metadata.heritage_book: Abbeville County bi-centennial, 1758-1958 (Abbeville County Historical Society, 1958)

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #201856. Will books, bonds, inventories, and appraisements from the Probate Court — one of the few record sets that survived all three courthouse fires. Critical primary source for Abbeville research.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived the 1872-1873 fires because probate records were housed separately from the main courthouse.

source_profiles.source_name: Abstracts of Old Ninety-Six and Abbeville District Wills and Bonds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #2044476. Published abstracts covering the district period. Essential companion to the original will books.

source_profiles.source_name: Equity Records of Old 96 and Abbeville District, Vol. 1, 1791-1849
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #637391. Equity court records survived the fires and serve as partial substitutes for destroyed deed records, as equity cases often reference property transactions, boundary disputes, and family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived the fires. Equity minutes and decree books (1820-1868) also survived.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1152450. Marriage registers that survived the courthouse fires.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived the fires.

source_profiles.source_name: Plats for State Land Grants 1784-1868 (Abbeville area)
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plats searchable by surname and placename at archivesindex.sc.gov. Specifically cited as a critical resource for Abbeville because the county lost its antebellum deed records in the fires.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-level records, not affected by county courthouse fires.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #443498. Loose estate papers from the probate court.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived the fires as part of probate holdings.

county_metadata.parent_county: Edgefield, Lexington, Barnwell, and Orangeburg (portions of all four)
county_metadata.record_losses: Original courthouse (built 1872 at 320 Colleton Avenue) burned in January 1970, destroying some early county records. Estate records ca. 1872-1927 are documented as surviving. Post-1970 records intact. Pre-1871 records for this area exist in parent counties Edgefield, Lexington, Barnwell, and Orangeburg.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871 from portions of four counties. Because it was formed from parts of Edgefield, Lexington, Barnwell, and Orangeburg, researchers must check all four parent counties for pre-1871 records. This is one of the more complex parent-county chains in SC.
county_metadata.research_tips: The four-parent formation makes Aiken tricky — you need to identify which portion of the county your ancestor's land was in to know which parent county's records to search. Estate records (catalog #983741, ca. 1872-1927) survived the 1970 fire. The Aiken-Barnwell Genealogical Society (aikenbarnwellgenealogy.net, PO Box 415, Aiken, SC 29802) is active and helpful. Aiken County Public Library has genealogical collections. For pre-1871 Edgefield records, note that Edgefield has one of the most complete record collections in SC (never burned, Sherman bypassed it).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tax assessor property records via qPublic (qpublic.net/sc/aiken/). No free online deed image portal found. In-person at Register of Deeds: 828 Richland Ave W, Aiken, SC 29801 (803-642-2072).
county_metadata.heritage_book: An Aiken scrapbook: a picture narrative of Aiken and Aiken County, South Carolina (Dorothy Kelly MacDowell, 1982)

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #983741. Estate records from the county's early decades. Survived the 1970 courthouse fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived the 1970 fire. Records from county formation (1871) through 1927.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Aiken County appears in federal census from 1880 (first census after 1871 formation). Pre-1871 census records for this area are under Edgefield, Lexington, Barnwell, or Orangeburg depending on location. 1890 census mostly destroyed nationwide.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records, unaffected by local courthouse fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of the statewide SC marriage index. Pre-1911 marriages must be searched in deed books, church records, or probate court records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-level index, not affected by local courthouse fire.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical Records Survey inventory of church records in Aiken County. Identifies which churches kept what records and their locations as of the late 1930s.

county_metadata.record_losses: Arson fire on May 18, 1998 destroyed most of the courthouse second-floor interior with extensive smoke and water damage. However, THREE fireproof vaults (Judge of Probate, Clerk of Court, Treasurer) protected all county records from destruction. No records were lost. Reconstruction began August 2002. This is a unique 'burned courthouse, saved records' case.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Last county created in South Carolina (February 4, 1919). Formed from southwestern Barnwell County along the Savannah River plus a portion of Hampton County. Pre-1919 records are in Barnwell County (primarily) and Hampton County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Despite the dramatic 1998 courthouse fire, Allendale's records survived in fireproof vaults. The real research challenge is that the county only existed from 1919 — all pre-1919 records are in Barnwell County. For cemetery research, use 'Barnwell County Cemeteries: Including Allendale and Bamberg' by Cyril Johnson (3 vols., 1999-2007). The Eighth Judicial Circuit Court Public Index covers Circuit, Summary, and Masters-in-Equity courts. For African American genealogy, check Freedmen's Bureau records for the Barnwell area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tax assessor records via qPublic (qpublic.net/sc/allendale/). No free online deed image portal. In-person: Clerk of Court/Register of Deeds, 292 Barnwell Highway, Allendale, SC (803-584-2737).
county_metadata.heritage_book: The town of Allendale: a gem of the South Carolina Low Country (Louis Arthur Searson, 1949)

source_profiles.repository: Allendale County Clerk of Court / Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Survived 1998 courthouse fire in fireproof vault. In-person access at 292 Barnwell Highway, Allendale, SC.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived 1998 fire in vault. Pre-1919 deeds are in Barnwell County.

source_profiles.source_name: SC Probate Records — Files and Loose Papers (includes Allendale area)
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Statewide probate records collection on FamilySearch that includes Allendale area records (filed under Barnwell pre-1919).

source_profiles.source_name: Barnwell County Cemeteries: Including Allendale and Bamberg
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Three-volume work by Cyril Johnson (1999-2007). Covers cemeteries across the Barnwell/Allendale/Bamberg area. FamilySearch catalog #376478 (Fairfax Cemetery, Allendale).
Allendale first appears in federal census in 1920 (formed 1919). Pre-1920 census records are under Barnwell County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records, unaffected by courthouse fire.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Anderson County has a well-preserved record collection.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created December 20, 1826 when Pendleton District was divided into Anderson District and Pickens District. Named for Revolutionary War leader Robert Anderson. Operated as a district until 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: Anderson has strong records and an active genealogical community. The Anderson County Library Local History & Genealogy section has family histories, census transcriptions, probate and deed abstracts, cemetery records, and military rosters. The Anderson County Register of Deeds (andersondeeds.com) was the FIRST in the country to offer online deed index access via internet (March 24, 1997) — records from September 1, 1968 through present with digital images. For pre-1826 records, check Pendleton District at SC Archives. FamilySearch has wills (catalog #308652, 1792-1857) covering both Old Pendleton District will books and Anderson District will books. The Anderson County Chapter of the SC Genealogical Society (andersoncounty.scgen.org) and the Old Pendleton District Chapter (covers Anderson, Oconee, Pickens) are both active.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Digital images from September 1, 1968 through present. Search interface at search.andersondeeds.com. First county ROD in the US to go online (1997).
county_metadata.heritage_book: Traditions and History of Anderson County (Louise Ayer Vandiver, 1928)

source_profiles.source_name: Anderson County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digital deed images from September 1968 through present. Search by name at search.andersondeeds.com. First county ROD in the US to offer online access (1997). Office: 100 S. Main Street, Room 101, Anderson, SC 29624 (864-260-4053).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete records from formation. No courthouse fires.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #308652. Vol. 1: Old Pendleton District Will Books C & A (1791-1834). Vol. 2: Anderson District Will Book B (1835-1857). Covers both the parent district and early county period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no record losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Anderson County Estate Papers and Index
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #967511 (estate papers packets) and #411210 (index to estate records). Loose papers from probate proceedings.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Early Anderson County Newspapers — Marriages, Obituaries (1841-1882)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Compiled marriages and obituaries extracted from early Anderson County newspapers. Also: Anderson Intelligencer (1860-1914).

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax books listing property owners, useful for tracking land ownership and residence. Also: Internal Revenue Assessment Lists 1864-1866 for the area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Anderson County Cemetery Records (including African American Cemeteries)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #376705 (general cemetery records) and #718615 (African American cemeteries). Also 'Book of the dead' catalog #2044468.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records begin with county formation in 1897.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created January 19, 1897 from the southeastern section of Barnwell County. Named for Francis Marion Bamberg (1838-1905). Pre-1897 records are in Barnwell County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bamberg is a young county (1897) so pre-formation research requires Barnwell County records. The Manning Collection of Barnwell County records (1717-1965, FamilySearch catalog #484480, available online) is essential for the Bamberg area's pre-1897 history. The 1790 Orangeburgh Census covers the broader area. Cemetery records are published in 'Barnwell County Cemeteries: Including Allendale and Bamberg' by Cyril Johnson (3 vols., 1999-2007). The Historical Society of Bamberg (1611 Heritage Highway, Bamberg, SC 29003) is a local resource.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tax assessor records via qPublic (qpublic.net/sc/bamberg/). Land records platform at bambergsc.avenuinsights.com. Register of Deeds/Clerk of Court: 803-245-3025 ext. 2109.

source_profiles.repository: Bamberg County Register of Deeds / Clerk of Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Documents include deeds, assignments, leases, liens, mortgages, plats, contracts, rights of way. Pre-1897 deeds are in Barnwell County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from formation. No major losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Manning Collection — Barnwell County Records (includes Bamberg area), 1717-1965
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #484480. Available online. Covers the broader Barnwell County area including what became Bamberg County. Essential for pre-1897 research.

source_profiles.source_name: Barnwell County Cemeteries: Including Allendale and Bamberg
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Three-volume work by Cyril Johnson (1999-2007). Comprehensive cemetery survey covering Bamberg area.
Bamberg first appears in federal census in 1900 (formed 1897). Pre-1897 census records are under Barnwell County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records.

county_metadata.record_losses: Early probate records (1781-1787) and clerk of court records including deeds (1791-1799) were destroyed with Orangeburg County records when they were burned in Columbia during Sherman's occupation in February 1865. The Orangeburg records had been moved to Columbia for safekeeping and were ironically consumed there. Conveyance books 1789-1945 survive on 102 microfilm reels at SC Archives.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Originally called Winton District, renamed Barnwell District 1798. Named for John Barnwell of Beaufort. Parent county for Bamberg (1897) and contributed to Allendale (1919). Pre-1798 records are in Orangeburg District.
county_metadata.research_tips: Despite the partial record loss, Barnwell has extensive surviving records. The Manning Collection (FamilySearch catalog #484480, 1717-1965, available online) is the essential starting point and covers the entire Barnwell/Bamberg/Allendale area. Birth records (catalog #383415, 1804-1958), wills (catalog #391698, 1779-1950), and inventories/appraisements/sale books (catalog #381700, 1808-1842) are all on FamilySearch. Conveyance books 1789-1945 on 102 microfilm reels at SC Archives provide deed coverage despite the losses. The Aiken-Barnwell Genealogical Society (aikenbarnwellgenealogy.net) covers this county. Online deeds at barnwelldeeds.com.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Name search and property description search available at barnwelldeeds.com.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Barnwell County Heritage (Barnwell County Heritage Book Committee, Walsworth Publishing, 1994)

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 102 microfilm reels of conveyance books. Bulk coverage 1789-1932. Some early deeds (1791-1799) from the destroyed Orangeburg records are not included.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deeds 1791-1799 were destroyed with Orangeburg records in Columbia, Feb 1865. Surviving books begin with partial coverage from 1789.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online search portal for Barnwell County deeds. Name search and property description search available.
FamilySearch catalog #484480. Available online. Comprehensive Barnwell County collection including records predating the county's formal existence.
FamilySearch catalog #391698. Will books spanning nearly the entire county history.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived despite the loss of some early probate records (1781-1787).

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #383415. Unusually early birth recording for SC (state didn't mandate until 1915). Includes delayed birth certificates.

source_profiles.source_name: Barnwell County Inventories, Appraisements and Sale Books, 1808-1842
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #381700. Estate inventories including property listings that often name enslaved persons.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original SC district (from parishes of Prince William, St. Luke, St. Helena, and St. Peter)
county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Records dating to colonial period (1660s+) are available. Beaufort has one of the better-preserved colonial record collections in SC.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original 1769 SC districts. Named for Henry Somerset, Duke of Beaufort. The Beaufort area includes the site of Santa Elena (1566-1587), one of the earliest European settlements in North America. Significant Gullah/Geechee cultural heritage in the Sea Islands. Port town founded 1672.
county_metadata.research_tips: Beaufort is a goldmine for colonial and African American genealogy. Start with FamilySearch wills and estate papers (catalog #456745, 1663-1978) — note the 1663 start date, among the earliest in SC. The 1755 tax list (catalog #1841359) is valuable for mid-colonial research. For Gullah/Geechee heritage, check Freedmen's Bureau marriage records (1866-1868) and Freedmen's Savings and Trust Company registers for the Beaufort Branch (1865-1874). The Beaufort County Historical Society (beaufortcountyhistoricalsociety.com, est. 1939) and the Beaufort District Collection (843-255-6446) are key local resources. St. Helena's Parish records (1725-1825) cover baptisms, marriages, and burials. The Santa Elena History Center in Beaufort has resources on the Spanish colonial period (1566-1587 settlement on Parris Island). Multiple online deed portals available: rod.beaufortcountysc.gov/searchng/ and sc-beaufort.publicaccessnow.com.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Legacy Register of Deeds search portal. Alternative portal: sc-beaufort.publicaccessnow.com. Official ROD page: beaufortcountysc.gov/register-of-deeds/.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The History of Beaufort County, South Carolina, Volume 1: 1514-1861 (Lawrence S. Rowland, Alexander Moore, George C. Rogers Jr., University of South Carolina Press, 1996)

source_profiles.source_name: Beaufort County Wills and Estate Papers, 1663-1978
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #456745. Among the earliest county-level records in SC with a 1663 start date. Covers over 300 years of wills and estate papers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Remarkably complete. No major fire losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Beaufort County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search portal. Alternative access: sc-beaufort.publicaccessnow.com/Searches/Real.aspx.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from colonial period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1841359. Mid-colonial tax list identifying property owners in the Beaufort district. Valuable census substitute.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Parish register covering baptisms, marriages, and burials. Includes French Huguenot settlement families. French version of Book of Common Prayer used 1706-1778.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Preserved.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage and divorce records created by the Freedmen's Bureau in Beaufort. Includes names of formerly enslaved couples, former slaveholders, and current residences. Critical for African American genealogy in the Sea Islands.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records.

source_profiles.source_name: Freedmen's Savings and Trust Company Registers, Beaufort Branch (1865-1874)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Bank account registers from the Beaufort branch of the Freedmen's Bank. Applications contain genealogical details: name, age, birthplace, residence, complexion, employer, family members, sometimes former enslaver.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records.

source_profiles.source_name: Beaufort County Court of Pleas and Quarter Sessions Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #358252. Court records from Beaufort's colonial/early period.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. County records begin with formation in 1882/1883.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Berkeley County has three historical incarnations: original 1682, 1685-1768 (abolished), 1785-1798 (non-functional), and the current county from January 31, 1882. Pre-1882 records are in Charleston County. Santee Cooper hydroelectric project (1939-1941) required grave relocations documented in FamilySearch catalog #449419.
county_metadata.research_tips: Berkeley was carved from Charleston in 1882, so pre-1882 research goes through Charleston County's extensive records. The Berkeley County deed search (search.berkeleydeeds.com) offers free access to documents from 1983-present with images, and deed images from January 1974 by book and page. The Berkeley County Museum (950 Stony Landing Road, Moncks Corner, 843-899-5101) and the Berkeley County Historical Society (PO Box 504, Moncks Corner, 843-276-6684) are local resources. St. James Parish Church Cemetery records at Goose Creek (catalog #156268) are valuable for colonial-era families. The Santee Cooper cemetery relocation records (catalog #449419, 1939-1941) document graves moved during the dam project.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Free online access. Documents from 1983-present with images. Deed images from January 1974 available by book and page number. Updates every 15 minutes.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Historic Berkeley County, South Carolina, 1671-1900 (Maxwell Clayton Orvin)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Free online access. Name search at search.berkeleydeeds.com/NameSearch.php. Documents indexed from July 1983; deed images from January 1974 by book/page. Updates every 15 minutes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from formation. Pre-1882 deeds in Charleston County.

source_profiles.source_name: St. James Parish Church Cemetery Records, Goose Creek
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #156268. St. James Goose Creek is one of the oldest churches in SC (colonial era). Cemetery records contain early settlement family data.
FamilySearch catalog #449419. Records of graves relocated during the Santee Cooper hydroelectric dam project. Documents identities and original burial locations of the relocated deceased.
FamilySearch catalog #233026. Early county administrative records from formation period.

county_metadata.record_losses: No record losses for the county itself (formed 1908). However, parent county Orangeburg lost records in the February 1865 Columbia burning, so pre-1908 records for this area may have gaps in the Orangeburg files.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created February 20, 1908 from Lexington and Orangeburg Counties. Named for John C. Calhoun. One of the youngest counties in SC. Pre-1908 records are split between Lexington and Orangeburg counties depending on which part of those counties the land was in.
county_metadata.research_tips: Calhoun is a young county (1908) so the real research challenge is navigating the pre-formation parent counties. Orangeburg records suffered losses in the 1865 Columbia burning, while Lexington lost pre-1839 clerk of court records (including deeds and probate) when federal troops advanced in February 1865. Marriage licenses available from July 1, 1911 (statewide requirement). The Calhoun County Museum (313 Butler Street, St. Matthews, 803-874-3964, Tue-Fri 9am-4pm, free admission) has an archive with genealogy materials including African American books, cemetery records, church records, court records, Jewish history materials, and Civil War items. Genealogy research by appointment. For online deed access, the Clerk of Court is at calhouncounty.sc.gov/clerk-court (803-874-3524).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: SC Public Index at publicindex.sccourts.org/calhoun/publicindex/ for court records. Deed records require subscription (TitleSearcher) or in-person visit. Clerk of Court: first floor of courthouse, M-F 9am-5pm.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Pre-1908 deeds are in Lexington and Orangeburg counties. In-person access at courthouse.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from formation. No losses.

source_profiles.source_name: SC Probate Records — Bound Volumes (includes Calhoun area via Orangeburg)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Statewide SC probate records bound volumes on FamilySearch. Calhoun area pre-1908 records filed under Orangeburg and Lexington.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Orangeburg records partially lost in 1865 Columbia burning.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Calhoun first appears in federal census in 1910 (formed 1908). Pre-1908 census records are under Orangeburg and Lexington counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records.

source_profiles.source_name: Calhoun County Museum Archive — Cemetery and Church Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Calhoun County Museum (313 Butler Street, St. Matthews) maintains an archive with cemetery books, church books, African American records, Jewish history materials, marriage/death/will books, and Civil War materials. Free admission. Research by appointment (803-874-3964).

county_metadata.record_losses: Charleston County preserved its records exceptionally well. Land record books date to 1719. Earliest conveyances from 1673-1719 are archived in Columbia at SC Archives. Probate records span 1671-1977 in bound volumes and 1732-1964 in files/loose papers. Among the most complete record collections in the American South.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original 1769 SC districts. Charleston, founded 1670, is one of the oldest European settlements in North America. The county's records are intertwined with colonial SC government records since Charleston was the colonial capital. Berkeley County was carved from Charleston in 1882.
county_metadata.research_tips: Charleston is the crown jewel of SC genealogy. The Register of Deeds has land record books from 1719 (charlestoncounty.org/departments/rod/rodsearch.php), with earlier conveyances (1673-1719) at SC Archives in Columbia. FamilySearch has an extraordinary range: land records 1719-1873 (catalog #23503), city directories 1782-1870 (catalog #2044617), probate account books 1843-1871 (catalog #419318), estate inventories 1732-1844, marriage settlements (films 22512-22520), deaths 1821-1886 (film 23361+), births 1877-1901 (film 23416+), and the 1868 voter registration roll (catalog #1321276). For African American genealogy, Charleston was the primary slave trade port in North America — 150,000-200,000 Africans entered through Charleston in nearly 1,000 cargos (1670-1808). Slave manifests are at NARA (RG 36), and coastwise manifests cover 1820-1858. The SC Historical Society (100 Meeting Street, est. 1855) has 30,000+ photos and 50,000+ publications. Church records are extraordinary: St. Philip's (1680s), St. Michael's (1751), French Huguenot Church (1687), Kahal Kadosh Beth Elohim (1749, oldest Reform Jewish congregation in continuous use in US). The International African American Museum's Center for Family History and the Avery Research Center (125 Bull Street) are essential for African American research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Document search portal with digital images. Coverage includes recent books through current. Alternative portal: sc-charleston.publicaccessnow.com. Pre-1719 conveyances at SC Archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #23503. Land record books covering 154 years of colonial and antebellum Charleston. Earliest county-held deeds. Pre-1719 conveyances (1673-1719) are at SC Archives in Columbia.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Remarkably complete. No major fire losses.

source_profiles.source_name: Charleston County Register of Deeds Online Search
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online document search with digital images. Alternative portal: sc-charleston.publicaccessnow.com/RealPropertyRecordSearch.aspx. Office: Charleston County Government Complex.

source_profiles.source_name: Charleston County Ordinary (Probate Judge) Account Books, 1843-1871
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #419318. Probate judge account books. Complements the broader SC Probate Records bound volumes (1671-1977) and files/loose papers (1732-1964) also on FamilySearch.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #2044617. City directories listing residents, occupations, and addresses. Invaluable for tracking urban families and newly arrived immigrants.

source_profiles.source_name: Coastwise Slave Manifests, Port of Charleston, 1820-1858
source_profiles.coverage_notes: U.S. Customs Service manifests documenting enslaved persons transported coastwise through Charleston. Each manifest includes: enslaved person's first name, sex, age, stature, shipper/enslaver name and residence. Charleston was the primary slave trade port in North America — 150,000-200,000 Africans in nearly 1,000 cargos (1670-1808). Available at NARA Atlanta (RG 36).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records. Complete for the documented period.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Philip's Church Records (est. 1680s) and St. Michael's Church Records (est. 1751)
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Historical Society / Individual churches
source_profiles.coverage_notes: St. Philip's: parish registers 1720-1758 and 1754-1810, church records 1810-1822. Oldest European-American congregation in SC (1680s). St. Michael's: church records and churchyard inscriptions, gravestone tablet list compiled 1906. Both microfilmed by FamilySearch.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Preserved. Among the most important colonial church records in America.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch Library film 23361+. City/county death records predating state registration (1915). Unusually early for SC.

source_profiles.source_name: Charleston County Birth Registers with Index, 1877-1901/1926
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch Library film 23416+. City/county birth registers with index, 1877-1901, with additional coverage to 1926. Predates state registration (1915).

FamilySearch catalog #1321276. Post-Civil War voter registration including newly enfranchised formerly enslaved men. Critical for African American genealogy — may be the first document listing freedmen by name in an official county record.
FamilySearch Library films 22512-22520. Marriage settlements (prenuptial agreements) were common among Charleston's wealthy families and contain detailed property and family information.
